	The Yaksha's 	3Test	SDG Promotes wisdom, fairness, ethical 16	decision-making, and justice.Wonder WindowThink about a time when you had to make an important decision or answer a difficult question. Discuss with your partner and write about the following:A situation when you had to think carefully before answeringWhat helped you decide the right answer:your	knowledge 	•			your	experience	•		advice	from	others calm	thinking 		•			honestyPeek InsideIn the forest, the thirsty Pandavas come across a mysterious lake guarded by a Yaksha. One by one they ignore his warning and fall unconscious. Let’s read to discover how Yudhishthira’s wisdom and calm thinking help him answer the Yaksha’s questions and save his brothers.Another time, it happened that the Pandava brothers, going in 1. pursuit: the act of following pursuit1 of a deer in the forest, wandered far from their dwelling or chasing something place. The Sun was hot overhead and they grew thirsty. The 2. weary: extremely tired deer had vanished, and now footsore and weary2 they came at last to a stop, knowing that their search was in vain. Their mouths were parched3; they scanned the earth in all directions for some sign of water. But seeing none, they trudged4 wearily onward until at last Yudhishthira, unable to go any further due to fatigue, sank down under a tree to rest and sent Sahadeva in search of water for them. The youth went readily enough, but when he did not return for a long time Yudhishthira grew concerned. He sent Nakula to see what had happened and also 3. parched: very dry to find water. Nakula 4. trudged: walked obeyed—but he too, like slowly and heavilySahadeva, did not return. Then Yudhishthira sent his brothers one by one after each other, but when they all went and none returned he grew very anxious, not knowing what evil fate had overtaken them. At last he decided to go and see for himself; he got up and following their footsteps, went down the track.The India BookmarkThe Mahabharata contains more than 100,000 verses, making it one of the longest epic in the world.He walked down for a distance until he came to a pool in a clearing in the forest. Its clear, still waters reflected the blue sky while the pink and white and blue lotuses that grew in it raised their smiling heads to the Sun.But as his eyes looked upon the lovely scene, Yudhishthira saw another sight that made his blood freeze in his veins. For upon the earth lay his four brothers, cold and still, either dead or unconscious. Their eyes were closed and when he called to them they would not answer. Sorrow filled the king’s heart, and he fell on his knees by them and wept bitterly for them, wringing5 his hands in his grief and crying out to heaven to take him too, for he could not live without his brothers. Now though Yudhishthira did not know it, this was what had happened: One by one his brothers had come to this forest pool. The first to come was Sahadeva. He was full of joy at seeing water, for he was very thirsty. He knelt upon the bank and bent down to drink the waters of the pool when a voice broke through the silence of the grove. ‘Stop Sahadeva!’ said the voice, ‘Do not drink, for this is an enchanted6 pool.’ Startled, Sahadeva looked about him, but he could see no one. He turned to the water. It looked cool and inviting. He was very thirsty. As he paused, undecided, the voice rang out again:‘I am a Yaksha7,’ it said ‘and, this enchanted pool belongs to me. No one may drink of its water until he has answered my questions.’Sahadeva rose up and waited. He could still see nobody. He seemed to be alone except for the birds chirping in the trees and the dragon flies zooming over the water.‘It must be my fancy,’ Sahadeva thought. ‘The hot Sun makes my imagination work.’He decided to ignore the voice of the invisible speaker. He was too tired and thirsty to wait. He made a cup of his hands and kneeling down began to drink.But hardly had the water touched his lips, when he felt himself struck down, Sahadeva’s senses reeled8. The world went dark and he Yaksha: a nature-spirit believed to be a caretaker of natural treasures according to Hindu mythology	fell down unconscious.reeled: seemed to revolveWhen Nakula arrived shortly afterwards he was grieved and surprised to see his brother in this condition. But so great was his own thirst that he did not wait. He rushed to the water’s edge to drink. But as he stooped, he too heard the Yaksha’s warning voice. However, he too paid no attention. He drank and instantly he too was struck down and rendered unconscious. When Bhima came to the spot, the same fate overtook him, though he was strong and mighty, and though he had sent a shower of arrows in all directions and searched all round for the hidden enemy.Arjuna fared no better, and when Yudhishthira came at last to the pool in search of them he found his four brothers lying as if they were ? What does this repeated mistake suggest dead. For a long time Yudhishthira wept over about how people sometimes behave in them. Then, feeling his thirst overpower moments of urgency or need? him, he dragged himself to the edge of the water. But as he bent down, Yudhishthira heard the Yaksha’s warning voice. Yudhishthira looked around. ‘Who are you?’ he asked. ‘Ask your questions and I shall answer them as well as I can.’Then the voice said: ‘I am a Yaksha, O Yudhishthira, and it is well for you that you heeded my warning. Now listen to my questions.’‘What makes the Sun shine?’‘The power of God!’ Yudhishthira answered.‘What is man’s surest weapon against danger?’ the Yaksha asked, and Yudhishthira said, ‘Courage! Courage is his surest weapon in danger.’“What gives more to man than even the earth does? What feeds him and sustains him and makes him strong?” the Yaksha questioned. Yudhishthira replied, “A mother, surely. It is only a mother who gives a man life and feeds him and sustains him. A mother is more than the earth.”“When does a man become loved by his fellows?” asked the Yaksha, and Yudhishthira returned, “When he gives up pride.” “What is that which makes a man happy when he has lost it?” the Yaksha went on. “Anger,” said Yudhishthira, without hesitation, for he knew that when a man gives up anger, then he is full of peace. The Yaksha continued:“What can a man give up and immediately become rich?”“Desire,” answered Yudhishthira, “it is only the man without desires who is really rich; but even if a man has a thousand things he will be poor if he is not satisfied.” So the Yaksha asked his questions, and Yudhishthira answered them until at last the Yaksha said, “O king, I am well pleased with your answers, and I shall restore to you one of your brothers. Choose who it shall be.”Yudhishthira looked at the unconscious forms of his brothers. It was hard for him to decide. But he spoke at last:‘O kind Yaksha,’ he said ‘Restore to me my brother Nakula.’ ‘And why Nakula?’ the Yaksha’s voice asked.‘Is not Bhima more useful to you? Will you not benefit from his great strength in the war that will surely come? And Arjuna—why do you not choose Arjuna? Is he not dearer to you than all? Is he not the most handsome, the most skilled among them all in the use of arms? Why then do you choose Nakula?’Then Yudhishthira gave his noblest answer, ‘Listen, O Yaksha,’ he said. ‘Righteousness and truth are a man’s only weapon and protection. The strength of Bhima and the skill of Arjuna would be of no use to me if I acted unrighteously. Indeed I would be unrighteous if I looked to my own gain and begged for Bhima’s life or Arjuna’s in preference to Nakula’s. For Nakula is Madri’s son, and Bhima and Arjuna, like me, are Kunti’s children. Of Kunti’s children I at least live. But if Nakula and Sahadeva should both die, then Madri’s line would end. Therefore, O Yaksha, it is right that Nakula’s life should be restored rather than Bhima’s or Arjuna’s.’When he had said this a wonderful thing happened. In a moment there appeared before Yudhishthira a shining, crowned person whom he knew at once to be a god, and no human being at all. Yudhishthira was right. The strange personage9 was none other than Yama, god of Justice and Death, Yudhishthira’s heavenly father. Yama embraced Yudhishthira and told him who he was, and how he had come to help the Pandavas in their hour of need. He told him how pleased he was with Yudhishthira’s noble conduct and wisdom, and he restored to life not just one of the brothers, but all of them. They arose and looked about them, dazed and unable to understand what had happened.But Yudhishthira knelt at his heavenly father’s feet and his heart overflowed with gratitude. Yama blessed him and promised that he and his brothers would be protected by heaven in their hardships.9. personage: an important or No harm shall come to you,’ he said. ‘Neither will you be famous person discovered while you live in hiding during the last and 10. exile: the state of being thirteenth year of exile10.’	banned from one’s native countryHe advised Yudhishthira to go with his What do you think would have happened brothers and Draupadi to Matsya, where the ? to the Pandavas if Yudhishthira had not good King Virata ruled, and to live there in been able to answer Yaksha’s questions? disguise. There they would be safe from their enemies while they awaited the end of their long period of exile. 11. dwelling: the place where someone livesThen Yama left Yudhishthira, who returned to his forest dwelling11 with his brothers.Who first went in search of water for the Pandavas?Bhima 	(b) Arjuna 	(c) Nakula 	(d) SahadevaWhich quality helped Yudhishthira answer the Yaksha’s questions?StrengthWealthWisdomSpeedWhich brother did Yudhishthira choose to bring back to life first?Arjuna 	(b) Bhima 	(c) Nakula 	(d) SahadevaWho was the Yaksha actually revealed to be?Indra 	(b) Yama 	(c) Vishnu 	(d) BrahmaWhat did the Yaksha do after hearing Yudhishthira’s wise answers?Punished him 	  	(b) Gave him treasure 	(c) Revived all his brothers 	(d) Sent him awayWrite ‘T’ for true and ‘F’ for false statements. The Pandavas were living in a palace when the incident took place. Sahadeva obeyed the Yaksha’s warning before drinking water. The Yaksha restored all the Pandavas to life at the end. Yudhishthira chose Bhima to be brought back to life first. All the brothers fell unconscious after ignoring the Yaksha’s warning. Arrange the following events in the order  (1-6) in which they happened in the chapter. Yudhishthira listens patiently to the Yaksha and answers all his questions  	 with wisdom. The Pandavas feel thirsty while wandering in the forest during exile. One by one, Yudhishthira’s brothers ignore the warning and fall unconscious  after drinking the water from the lake. Yudhishthira reaches the lake and sees all his brothers lying motionless on  the ground. A mysterious Yaksha warns the brothers not to drink the water before  answering his questions. Pleased with Yudhishthira’s wisdom and sense of justice, the Yaksha  brings all the Pandavas back to life. D. Answer the following questions: What happened when Nakul, Sahadeva, Arjuna, and Bhima went to the lake  one after another?Why did Yudhishthira succeed where his brothers failed at the lake?Why is Yudhishthira’s choice of Nakula considered just and fair?“Yudhishthira remained calm and thoughtful even in a difficult situation.” Elaborate.What was revealed about the Yaksha at the end of the story?What questions did the Yaksha ask, and how did Yudhishthira handle them?	Put on Your Thinking Caps	 	/Yudhishthira made a difficult but fair choice when asked to revive one brother. Now imagine you are in a similar dilemma. You are given three situations below. For each: Analyse the situation, take a decision and justify your final choice in 3–4 lines.You can save either your best friend or a stranger who has done great service to society.You must choose between telling the truth (which may hurt someone) or hiding it to keep peace.You can win a competition unfairly or lose honestly.	Dictionary & Vocabulary Skills	  	/Use a dictionary to arrange the following words in alphabetical order. Then write the page number on which each word appears in your dictionary. virtue,         wisdom,         justice,         honesty,         judgement,         knowledgeComplete the following table with words from the text in Column 2 for the given meanings in Column 1.MeaningsWords1.  Very tired2.  Extremely dry with thirst3.  Magical or under a spell4.  A place where one lives5.  Feeling worried or uneasy	Grammar Lab	  	/SentencesA sentence is a group of words that expresses a complete thought.But sentences are not all the same—they serve different purposes. Based on their purpose, sentences are divided into different kinds.Assertive Sentences– These sentences state a fact, opinion, or idea. 	 They can be affirmative or negative. 	 Example: • Wisdom leads to better decisions. 	• She did not know the answers. Interrogative Sentences–  These sentences ask questions and end with a question mark (?).  Example: • What is the correct answer?  • Did he understand the situation?  3. Imperative Sentences– These sentences give orders, instructions, requests, or advice. 	 Example: • Think carefully before you answer. 	• Do not ignore important details. 4. Exclamatory Sentences– These sentences express strong emotions like surprise, joy, anger, or admiration. They end with an exclamation mark (!) 	 Example: • What a wise decision that was!  	• How surprising the answer is!Rewrite the following as directed:He gave a correct answer. (Change into interrogative)Think before you speak. (Change into assertive)What a difficult question it is! (Change into assertive)She understood the situation. (Change into exclamatory)Do not waste time. (Change into assertive)Scan & LearnScan the QR code to access Ask questions, clear yout the worksheet for this doubts, and learn more with chapter.AI.
